q: A macromolecule is a very large molecule that contains thousands of atoms or more.
Carbohydrates, proteins, and nucleic acids are natural polymers.
Plastics, fibres, and elastomers are synthetic macromolecules manufactured in industry.
A polymer consists of only one kind of monomers is called a copolymer.
The process of joining together a large number of monomers to form a polymer is called polymerisation.
Addition polymerisation occurs when unsaturated monomers join together without losing any molecules or atoms.
Condensation polymerisation occurs with the removal of a small molecule such as water.
The simplest addition polymer is polyethene produced by the addition polymerisation of ethene monomers.
In polyethene, the carbon-carbon double bonds of the ethene molecules break to form single bonds with other monomers.
Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) is produced from monomers of chloroethene.
Addition polymers are generally soluble in water at low temperatures.
Teflon is heat-resistant and has non-stick properties.
Polystyrene is used to make disposable containers.
Condensation polymerisation is characterised by monomers that contain two different functional groups.
Terylene is an example of a polyester because it contains many ester linkages.
Nylon is the first synthetic fibre made from a dicarboxylic acid and a diamine.
Nylon is a polyamide because it contains many amide linkages.
Proteins are made from thousands of amino acid monomers.
The bond formed between two amino acids is called a peptide bond (amide link).
Condensation polymers often have lower molecular masses than addition polymers.
Plastics are biodegradable and can be easily broken down by bacteria in the soil.
PVC produces corrosive hydrogen chloride gas during combustion.
Biodegradable plastics contain small amounts of starch granules to help them decompose naturally.
Photodegradable plastics incorporate carbonyl groups ($C=O$) that absorb energy from ultraviolet rays to break down.
The number 1 recycling symbol stands for polyethene terephthalate (PETE/PET).
